front: Define an inner product on a real vector space V
back: A function ⟨·,·⟩: V × V → ℝ that is: (1) Bilinear (linear in each argument), (2) Symmetric: ⟨u,v⟩ = ⟨v,u⟩, (3) Positive definite: ⟨v,v⟩ ≥ 0 with equality only when v = 0

front: What is the standard inner product (dot product) in ℝⁿ?
back: For vectors x = (x₁,...,xₙ) and y = (y₁,...,yₙ), the dot product is x·y = Σᵢ xᵢyᵢ = x₁y₁ + x₂y₂ + ... + xₙyₙ
For any vectors u,v in an inner product space: |⟨u,v⟩| ≤ ||u|| ||v||, with equality iff u and v are linearly dependent.

front: State the triangle inequality for inner product spaces
back: For any vectors u,v: ||u + v|| ≤ ||u|| + ||v||. Equality holds when u and v are in the same direction (positive scalar multiples).

front: Define the norm (length) induced by an inner product
back: For any vector v in an inner product space: ||v|| = √⟨v,v⟩. This satisfies all norm axioms: positivity, absolute homogeneity, triangle inequality.

front: When are two vectors orthogonal in an inner product space?
back: Vectors u and v are orthogonal if ⟨u,v⟩ = 0. Denoted u ⊥ v. The zero vector is orthogonal to every vector.

front: What is a unit vector? How do you normalize a vector?
back: A unit vector has norm equal to 1: ||v|| = 1. To normalize v (where v ≠ 0), divide by its norm: v̂ = v/||v||.

front: Define an orthonormal set of vectors
back: A set of vectors {v₁,...,vₖ} is orthonormal if each vector is a unit vector (||vᵢ|| = 1) and any two distinct vectors are orthogonal (⟨vᵢ,vⱼ⟩ = 0 for i ≠ j).

front: What is the Pythagorean theorem in inner product spaces?
back: If u ⊥ v, then ||u + v||² = ||u||² + ||v||². More generally, for orthogonal vectors: ||Σᵢ vᵢ||² = Σᵢ ||vᵢ||².
A square matrix Q is orthogonal if Q⁻¹ = Qᵀ. Equivalently, its columns form an orthonormal basis. Property: Q preserves inner products and norms.
For any vectors u,v in an inner product space: ||u + v||² + ||u - v||² = 2(||u||² + ||v||²). This characterizes norms that come from inner products.
A method to convert a linearly independent set into an orthogonal (or orthonormal) set. For each new vector, subtract its projections onto all previously computed orthogonal vectors.

front: Define the angle between two non-zero vectors
back: The angle θ between vectors u and v is given by: cos θ = ⟨u,v⟩ / (||u|| ||v||), where 0 ≤ θ ≤ π. This follows from Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.

front: What is a weighted inner product?
back: For a positive definite weight matrix W: ⟨u,v⟩_W = uᵀWv = ΣᵢΣⱼ wᵢⱼuᵢvⱼ. Commonly with diagonal weights: ⟨u,v⟩_w = Σᵢ wᵢuᵢvᵢ.

front: What is an inner product on function space?
back: For continuous functions on [a,b]: ⟨f,g⟩ = ∫ₐᵇ f(x)g(x)dx. This satisfies all inner product axioms including positive definiteness (∫ f² ≥ 0, = 0 only if f = 0).
